is a New Zealand parliamentary [electorate](/wiki/New_Zealand_electorates "New Zealand electorates"), returning one Member of Parliament to the [New Zealand House of Representatives](/wiki/New_Zealand_House_of_Representatives "New Zealand House of Representatives"). It was first established in 1919, and has existed continuously since 1954. The current MP for Rotorua is [Todd McClay](/wiki/Todd_McClay "Todd McClay") of the [National Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_National_Party "New Zealand National Party"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) who won the electorate in the [2008 general election](/wiki/2008_New_Zealand_general_election "2008 New Zealand general election") from incumbent [Labour](/wiki/New_Zealand_Labour_Party "New Zealand Labour Party") MP [Steve Chadwick](/wiki/Steve_Chadwick "Steve Chadwick").

**Rotorua** is a New Zealand parliamentary [electorate](/wiki/New_Zealand_electorates "New Zealand electorates"), returning one Member of Parliament to the [New Zealand House of Representatives](/wiki/New_Zealand_House_of_Representatives "New Zealand House of Representatives"). It was first established in 1919, and has existed continuously since 1954. The current MP for Rotorua is [Todd McClay](/wiki/Todd_McClay "Todd McClay") of the [National Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_National_Party "New Zealand National Party"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) who won the electorate in the [2008 general election](/wiki/2008_New_Zealand_general_election "2008 New Zealand general election") from incumbent [Labour](/wiki/New_Zealand_Labour_Party "New Zealand Labour Party") MP [Steve Chadwick](/wiki/Steve_Chadwick "Steve Chadwick").

## Population centres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua_(electorate)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Population centres")]

In the 1918 electoral redistribution, the [North Island](/wiki/North_Island "North Island") gained a further three electorates from the [South Island](/wiki/South_Island "South Island") due to faster population growth. Only two existing electorates were unaltered, five electorates were abolished, two former electorate were re-established, and three electorates, including Rotorua, were created for the first time.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcRobie198975–80-3)

The initial electorate, which was formed through the 1918 electoral redistribution, had a long coastline along the [Bay of Plenty](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty "Bay of Plenty"), and incorporated, beside Rotorua, the towns and villages of [Whakatāne](/wiki/Whakat%C4%81ne "Whakatāne"), [Taupō](/wiki/Taup%C5%8D "Taupō"), [Tokoroa](/wiki/Tokoroa "Tokoroa"), [Putāruru](/wiki/Put%C4%81ruru "Putāruru"), [Mangakino](/wiki/Mangakino "Mangakino"), [Edgecumbe](/wiki/Edgecumbe "Edgecumbe"), [Tāneatua](/wiki/T%C4%81neatua "Tāneatua"), and [Murupara](/wiki/Murupara "Murupara").[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcRobie198978f-4) In the 1922 electoral redistribution, the electorate lost some area to the [Bay of Plenty](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Bay of Plenty (New Zealand electorate)") electorate, and a larger area to the [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Waikato (New Zealand electorate)") electorate.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcRobie198982f-5) The 1927 electoral redistribution saw Rotorua become landlocked, with the [Tauranga](/wiki/Tauranga_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Tauranga (New Zealand electorate)") electorate taking the coastline including Tāneatua and Edgecumbe, and Whakatāne going to the Bay of Plenty electorate. The electorate moved south and took in [Lake Taupō](/wiki/Lake_Taup%C5%8D "Lake Taupō"), with [Tūrangi](/wiki/T%C5%ABrangi "Tūrangi") just beyond the southern boundary located in the [Waimarino](/wiki/Waimarino_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Waimarino (New Zealand electorate)") electorate. The electorate also grew in the north-west, gaining the town of [Matamata](/wiki/Matamata "Matamata").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcRobie198986f-6)

In the 1937 electoral redistribution, the electorate shifted further south again. Matamata was lost again, and the peaks of [Tongariro](/wiki/Mount_Tongariro "Mount Tongariro"), [Ngauruhoe](/wiki/Mount_Ngauruhoe "Mount Ngauruhoe"), and [Ruapehu](/wiki/Mount_Ruapehu "Mount Ruapehu") now formed the boundary to the Waimarino electorate.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcRobie198990f-7) The 1946 electoral redistribution saw the Rotorua electorate abolished, with the Bay of Plenty electorate moving west and incorporating the town of Rotorua, most of the southern area going to the Waimarino electorate including the town of Taupō, and some area in the north-west going to the Waikato electorate including Tokoroa.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcRobie198994f-8)

The [First Labour Government](/wiki/First_Labour_Government_of_New_Zealand "First Labour Government of New Zealand") was defeated in the [1949 election](/wiki/1949_New_Zealand_general_election "1949 New Zealand general election") and the incoming [National Government](/wiki/First_National_Government_of_New_Zealand "First National Government of New Zealand") changed the Electoral Act, with the electoral quota once again based on total population as opposed to qualified electors, and the tolerance was increased to 7.5% of the electoral quota. There was no adjustments in the number of electorates between the [South](/wiki/South_Island "South Island") and [North Islands](/wiki/North_Island "North Island"), but the law changes resulted in boundary adjustments to almost every electorate through the 1952 electoral redistribution; only five electorates were unaltered.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcRobie198999f-9) Five electorates were reconstituted (including Rotorua) and one was newly created, and a corresponding six electorates were abolished; all of these in the North Island.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcRobie198995–100-10) These changes took effect with the [1954 election](/wiki/1954_New_Zealand_general_election "1954 New Zealand general election").[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcRobie198999-11) The electorate was again landlocked and much smaller than prior to its abolition. Significant settlements included Rotorua, Tokoroa, Taupō, and Mangakino, with Lake Taupō forming the southern boundary.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcRobie198990,_98-12)

The current Rotorua electorate is positioned in the [Bay of Plenty](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty "Bay of Plenty") region in the central North Island. It is dominated by the town of [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua"), and also contains the Eastern Bay of Plenty towns of [Kawerau](/wiki/Kawerau "Kawerau"), [Murupara](/wiki/Murupara "Murupara") and [Galatea](/wiki/Galatea,_New_Zealand "Galatea, New Zealand"), the last two of which are located on the outskirts of [Te Urewera National Park](/wiki/Te_Urewera_National_Park "Te Urewera National Park"). In 2008, its boundaries were extended to the geographical bay, with the addition of coastline stretching from a cluster of rural towns including Pukehina and [Maketu](/wiki/Maketu "Maketu") to the outskirts of [Te Puke](/wiki/Te_Puke "Te Puke"). In the 2025 boundary review, the electorate expanded northward to the outskirts of [Tauranga](/wiki/Tauranga "Tauranga") with the [Tauranga Eastern Link](/wiki/Tauranga_Eastern_Link "Tauranga Eastern Link") forming part of the northern boundary. Initial proposals saw the rural towns of the [Kaimai Range](/wiki/Kaimai_Range "Kaimai Range") added to the electorate, though this was reverted after public consultation.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua_(electorate)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Over forty per cent of the population of Rotorua is under the age of thirty, much of this because 37% of the electorate's residents are [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people"), who are on the whole younger than the national average (22.7 years old versus a national average of 35.9).[[14]](#cite_note-14) There are also fewer voters earning over $30,000 per year, with the majority of workers coming from working class and semi-skilled professionals. Rotorua also has more unemployed people (6.5%) than most electorates, being ranked 52nd in the nation.

The [country quota](/wiki/Country_quota "Country quota") applied until 1945 and the Rotorua electorate was initially classed as fully rural. Based on the [1926 New Zealand census](/wiki/1926_New_Zealand_census "1926 New Zealand census"), the 1927 Electoral Redistribution determined that 24% of the electorate's population was urban. Based on the 1936 census, the 1937 Electoral Redistribution determined that 36% of the electorate's population was urban.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcRobie198979–95-15)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rotorua_(electorate)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

An electorate based around Rotorua has been a part of the New Zealand electoral landscape since the [1919 election](/wiki/1919_New_Zealand_general_election "1919 New Zealand general election"), with a gap from [1946](/wiki/1946_New_Zealand_general_election "1946 New Zealand general election") to 1954. Previously the town of Rotorua was in the [East Coast](/wiki/East_Coast_(New_Zealand_electorate) "East Coast (New Zealand electorate)") electorate (from [1871](/wiki/1871_New_Zealand_general_election "1871 New Zealand general election")), then the East Coast electorate again (from [1890](/wiki/1890_New_Zealand_general_election "1890 New Zealand general election")), then the [Bay of Plenty](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Bay of Plenty (New Zealand electorate)") electorate (from [1893](/wiki/1893_New_Zealand_general_election "1893 New Zealand general election")), and then (just) in the Tauranga electorate again (from [1911](/wiki/1911_New_Zealand_general_election "1911 New Zealand general election") to 1919).[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcRobie198938–75-16)

William Henry Wackrow was nominated in March 1922 as the [opposition](/wiki/New_Zealand_Liberal_Party "New Zealand Liberal Party") candidate for that [year's election](/wiki/1922_New_Zealand_general_election "1922 New Zealand general election").[[17]](#cite_note-17) Wackrow withdrew in November[[18]](#cite_note-18) and was replaced by [Cecil Clinkard](/wiki/Cecil_Clinkard "Cecil Clinkard"), who lost against the incumbent, [Frank Hockly](/wiki/Frank_Hockly "Frank Hockly") of the [Reform Party](/wiki/Reform_Party_(New_Zealand) "Reform Party (New Zealand)").[[19]](#cite_note-1922_election-19)

[Geoffrey Sim](/wiki/Geoffrey_Sim "Geoffrey Sim") of the [National Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_National_Party "New Zealand National Party") won the [1943 election](/wiki/1943_New_Zealand_general_election "1943 New Zealand general election"). When the Rotorua electorate was abolished for the [1946 election](/wiki/1946_New_Zealand_general_election "1946 New Zealand general election"), Sim successfully stood in Waikato electorate instead.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson1985234-20)

After the electorate was re-established through the 1952 Electoral Redistribution, [Ray Boord](/wiki/Ray_Boord "Ray Boord") of the Labour Party won the [1954 election](/wiki/1954_New_Zealand_general_election "1954 New Zealand general election").[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson1985184-21) Boord served two parliamentary terms and was beaten by National's [Harry Lapwood](/wiki/Harry_Lapwood "Harry Lapwood") in the [1960 election](/wiki/1960_New_Zealand_general_election "1960 New Zealand general election").[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson1985184,_211-22) Lapwood served for six parliamentary terms and retired in 1978.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson1985211-23)

Lapwood was succeeded by his party colleague [Paul East](/wiki/Paul_East "Paul East") in the [1978 election](/wiki/1978_New_Zealand_general_election "1978 New Zealand general election"). East also served six parliamentary terms until 1996. With the advent of [Mixed Member Proportional](/wiki/Mixed-member_proportional_representation "Mixed-member proportional representation") (MMP) voting in [1996](/wiki/1996_New_Zealand_general_election "1996 New Zealand general election"), the Rotorua electorate was greatly expanded to include areas previously part of the [Eastern Bay of Plenty](/wiki/Eastern_Bay_of_Plenty_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Eastern Bay of Plenty (New Zealand electorate)") and [Tarawera](/wiki/Tarawera_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Tarawera (New Zealand electorate)") electorates. Both Tarawera and Rotorua were safe National Party electorates, and in the ensuing battle for the nomination, the two incumbents, East and [Max Bradford](/wiki/Max_Bradford "Max Bradford"), faced off for a Rotorua nomination eventually secured by Bradford, with East securing a high [list position](/wiki/Party_lists_in_the_1996_New_Zealand_general_election#National_Party "Party lists in the 1996 New Zealand general election").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Bradford won the 1996 election with a nearly 6,000 votes margin.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Despite both electorates being reasonably loyal to the National Party, Bradford's tenure as MP for Rotorua was just three years, before being ousted by Labour MP [Steve Chadwick](/wiki/Steve_Chadwick "Steve Chadwick") in the [1999 election](/wiki/1999_New_Zealand_general_election "1999 New Zealand general election"). Chadwick's initial majority of 4,978 votes blew out to over 7,500 in [2002](/wiki/2002_New_Zealand_general_election "2002 New Zealand general election") before it was reined in to just 662 in [2005](/wiki/2005_New_Zealand_general_election "2005 New Zealand general election"), as the National Party consolidated the centre-right vote, with its biggest gains being in the provincial North Island. In 2005, Chadwick's party was less popular than their candidate, coming 1,645 votes behind National.

In 2008 Chadwick was defeated by National candidate [Todd McClay](/wiki/Todd_McClay "Todd McClay") who won the electorate with a majority of 5,067 votes. In the 2011 election McClay again returned as the member for Rotorua, increasing his majority to 7,357 votes. In 2014, McClay was elected as MP for a third term beating television personality [Tāmati Coffey](/wiki/T%C4%81mati_Coffey "Tāmati Coffey") by a similar majority to that in the previous election.

Rotorua is also an electorate where the [New Zealand First](/wiki/New_Zealand_First "New Zealand First") party does well, with its biggest appeal among provincial New Zealanders, and as results in [1996](/wiki/1996_New_Zealand_general_election "1996 New Zealand general election") indicate, Māori: in the three most recent elections, New Zealand First has polled around three per cent higher in Rotorua than it did in the rest of New Zealand.
